reasoning: The Lean statement `∀ n : ℕ, ∃ N : ℤ, N > n ∧ ...` is the standard Lean idiom for "unbounded above in ℕ", which is equivalent to "infinitely many" here because the set of valid N is bounded below by 0 (sums of squares are non-negative), and for any bounded-below set of integers, infinite ↔ unbounded above. The use of `i : Fin 6 → ℕ` to encode "sum of two squares of integers" is equivalent since for any integer k, k² = (|k|)², so sums of two integer squares and sums of two natural-number squares are the same set of integers. The types (N : ℤ matching "integer n" in the informal) and the three conditions on N, N+1, N+2 match precisely. No hypotheses are silently added or dropped.
